glossary.def: An encoder-decoder architecture in which one network reads a variable-length input sequence into a state and a second network generates a variable-length output sequence from it. Introduced for machine translation, it underlies summarization, dialogue, captioning, and speech-to-text.

The summary the encoder hands to the decoder. In plain seq2seq it is a single fixed-length vector (the encoder's last state); with attention it is recomputed at every output step as a weighted sum of all encoder states.

The failure mode of plain seq2seq: an entire source sentence must be compressed into one constant-width vector, so translation quality degrades as input length grows. Attention removes it by letting the decoder read all encoder states on demand.

A differentiable soft lookup in which a query is scored against a set of keys, the scores are softmaxed into weights that sum to one, and the output is the weighted sum of the corresponding values. Born in seq2seq translation as cross-attention between decoder and encoder.

The original 2014 attention: a small feedforward network scores each encoder annotation against the decoder state via v·tanh(W·s + U·h). More robust when query and key dimensions differ, at the cost of an MLP per pair.

The 2015 simplification that scores alignment with a dot product (sᵀh), an optional learned matrix (sᵀW·h, the 'general' form), or a concat MLP. Faster and more memory-efficient; its scaled 'general' form became scaled dot-product attention in the Transformer.

The learned scorer that decides how much each source position contributes to the current output step. It is trained jointly with the translator from the translation loss alone — alignment emerges, it is never supervised.

The softmax-normalized alignment scores giving, for output step i, a probability distribution over the source positions j. They sum to one, making the context vector a convex combination — a true weighted average — of the encoder states.

refs.cite: Sutskever, I., Vinyals, O. & Le, Q. V. (2014). Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks. NeurIPS 2014.
Bahdanau, D., Cho, K. & Bengio, Y. (2014). Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate. ICLR 2015.
Luong, M.-T., Pham, H. & Manning, C. D. (2015). Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation. EMNLP 2015.
Cho, K. et al. (2014). Learning Phrase Representations using RNN Encoder-Decoder for Statistical Machine Translation. EMNLP 2014.
Vaswani, A. et al. (2017). Attention Is All You Need. NeurIPS 2017.
Xu, K. et al. (2015). Show, Attend and Tell: Neural Image Caption Generation with Visual Attention. ICML 2015.
